items.mistake: Saying "accept H₀" when the p-value exceeds α
items.fix: Say "fail to reject H₀"—absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
items.why: Tests are designed to guard against false positives when H₀ is true; they are not proofs that H₀ holds.
items.example: p = 0.12 for H₀: μ = 0 does not mean μ is exactly 0; a wide CI like (−0.5, 2.1) may still include meaningful effects.

items.mistake: Interpreting p-value as P(H₀ is true | data)
items.fix: Remember p = P(data this extreme | H₀ true). Use Bayes factors or prior beliefs for P(H₀ | data).
items.why: The definition is conditional on H₀ being true; reversing it requires additional assumptions.
items.example: p = 0.03 means "if there were truly no effect, such data would occur ~3% of the time"—not "3% chance the null is true."

items.mistake: Choosing one-sided vs two-sided after seeing which direction the effect went
items.fix: Commit to one- or two-sided tests before data collection based on whether only one direction is scientifically meaningful.
items.why: Post-hoc one-sided testing effectively doubles α for the observed direction and inflates false positives.
items.example: Observing x̄ > μ₀ then switching to H₁: μ > μ₀ to halve the p-value is not valid unless that direction was pre-registered.

items.fix: Check independence, approximate Normality of data or sample mean (CLT), and variance homogeneity for pooled two-sample tests; use Welch or nonparametric alternatives when violated.
items.why: Invalid p-values follow from wrong reference distributions.
items.example: Heavy-tailed latency spikes with n=15 may need a log transform or Mann–Whitney instead of a plain t-test.
